findings.summary: The three-package decomposition (common, ocr, classifier) maps cleanly to domain capabilities with clear ownership boundaries
evidence: common/ contains shared infrastructure (config, paperless client, retry, tags, daemon loop), ocr/ contains vision-LLM transcription, classifier/ contains document classification - each package has a coherent purpose
findings.suggestion: No change needed - this is a positive observation

findings.summary: Settings class mixes OCR-specific, classifier-specific, and shared config into a single 80+ field monolith
evidence: Settings has CLASSIFY_* fields (8 fields), OCR_* fields (4 fields), and shared fields all in one class; both daemons load all config regardless of which one is running; the class spans 250 lines with 7 private loader methods
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting into BaseSettings + OcrSettings + ClassifierSettings, or at minimum group with nested dataclasses to reflect the domain ownership boundaries

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon uses stringly-typed getattr to access settings attributes, obscuring the contract
evidence: recover_stale_locks call uses getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr) and getattr(settings, pre_tag_id_attr) where the callers pass string attribute names like 'OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' - this loses type safety and makes the dependency invisible
findings.suggestion: Accept the tag IDs directly as int parameters instead of attribute name strings, letting each daemon pass its specific values

findings.summary: Each document creates a new PaperlessClient (HTTP session) instead of reusing the listing client
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 25: PaperlessClient(settings) created per-document in _process_document; classifier/daemon.py line 67: same pattern; the list_client is already available and thread-safe via httpx.Client
findings.suggestion: httpx.Client is thread-safe; consider reusing the existing client or using a connection pool instead of constructing a new HTTP session per document

findings.summary: OpenAIProvider and ClassificationProvider duplicate the stats tracking pattern (lock, dict, _inc_stat, get_stats) with no shared abstraction
evidence: ocr/provider.py lines 83-101: _stats_lock, _stats dict, _inc_stat, get_stats; classifier/provider.py lines 28-56: identical pattern with _stats_lock, _stats dict, _inc_stat, get_stats; both use threading.Lock with the same increment/snapshot logic
findings.suggestion: Extract a small StatsCounter helper into common/ or add the stats tracking to OpenAIChatMixin since both providers inherit from it

findings.summary: The retry decorator silently requires self.settings to satisfy RetrySettings protocol, creating an invisible coupling
evidence: retry.py line 33: 'settings: RetrySettings = self.settings' assumes the decorated method's owner has a settings attribute; this contract is documented in a docstring but not enforced or visible at the call site; PaperlessClient and OpenAIChatMixin both silently depend on this
findings.suggestion: Consider making the retry settings explicit via a parameter or a base class/protocol that declares the settings attribute

findings.summary: OcrProvider ABC has only one concrete implementation (OpenAIProvider) and no extension pressure
evidence: OcrProvider ABC defined at line 46 with abstractmethod transcribe_image; only OpenAIProvider implements it; no plugin system or factory; the docstring says 'Subclass this to add support for a new vision-capable LLM backend' but no second backend exists
findings.suggestion: The ABC adds minimal overhead here, but if no second provider is planned it could be simplified to just OpenAIProvider

findings.summary: PaperlessClient._create_named_item has convoluted matching_algorithm candidate logic for a simple compatibility concern
evidence: Lines 63-100: builds a candidates list with None/str/int permutations, then loops through them catching 400 errors; the branching on isinstance(matching_algorithm, str) vs int with different fallback orders is hard to follow; the method also has an implicit non-return path (if last_response is not checked after the loop)
findings.suggestion: Simplify by always trying both representations in a fixed order, or handle the compatibility at a higher level (TaxonomyCache._infer_matching_algorithm already does this)

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat has a repetitive if/elif/elif ladder for stripping parameters on 400 errors
evidence: Lines 104-149: three nearly identical blocks each check a predicate, log a warning, increment a stat, pop a param, increment compat_retries, and continue; the only differences are the param name, the predicate method, and the stat key
findings.suggestion: Extract a list of (predicate, param_name, stat_key) tuples and iterate over them, reducing ~30 lines to ~10

findings.summary: Both worker process() methods combine refresh, validation, lock acquisition, processing, and cleanup in a single long method with deep nesting
evidence: ocr/worker.py process() spans lines 53-131 with nested try/finally blocks and multiple early returns; classifier/worker.py process() spans lines 70-142 with similar structure; both have the pattern: try → refresh → check error → claim → [domain work] → finally release
findings.suggestion: The shared try/claim/finally pattern could be extracted into a context manager or template method in common/, letting workers focus on just the domain-specific steps

findings.summary: Tag set manipulation (add/discard/clean pipeline tags) is spread across multiple callsites with no centralized state machine
evidence: ocr/worker.py lines 231-234: manual discard PRE, discard PROCESSING, add POST; classifier/worker.py lines 296-299: similar manual tag arithmetic; common/tags.py clean_pipeline_tags removes a hardcoded list of tag IDs; finalize_document_with_error does its own version; the tag lifecycle transitions are implicit and easy to get wrong
findings.suggestion: Introduce a TagTransition helper or state machine that encodes the valid transitions (queued -> processing -> done, queued -> error) and applies them atomically

findings.summary: OCR refusal markers are defined in both Settings (OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS) and classifier/constants.py (ERROR_PHRASES) with overlapping content
evidence: config.py lines 113-119: default_ocr_refusal_markers includes 'i can't assist', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe'; constants.py lines 55-61: ERROR_PHRASES includes the same phrases; these are checked independently by ocr/provider.py is_refusal() and classifier/quality_gates.py needs_error_tag()
findings.suggestion: Define the canonical refusal phrases once in common/ and let both pipelines reference the same source; the configurable OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS can extend (not duplicate) the base set
